content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreements

### Verse 1
| Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Opening conjunction | "Christos therefore having suffered" | "Therefore, since Christ suffered" | "Since Christ suffered" | **LOGOS** — "Therefore, since Christ suffered" preserves both the conjunction and readable English word order. EMET's participial construction is too stilted; PNEUMA drops "therefore." |
| "arm yourselves with the same mind" | "arm yourselves [with] the same mind" | "arm yourselves also with the same mind" | "arm yourselves with the same attitude he had" | **LOGOS** — retains "also" (kai) from the Greek. PNEUMA adds "he had" (interpretive expansion not in the text). |
| "has ceased from sin" | "has ceased from sin" | "has ceased from sin" | "you have finished with sin" | **EMET/LOGOS** — all three convey the meaning, but PNEUMA shifts to second person ("you") which is interpretive. The Greek is third-person singular. |

### Verse 2
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "desires" vs "lusts" | "desires of men" | "lusts of men" | "human desires" | **EMET** — "desires" for epithumiais; "lusts" is reserved for contexts with stronger sexual connotation. Both are valid renderings. |
| Construction | "so as to live the remaining time in [the] flesh no longer by [the] desires" | "so as to live the remaining time in the flesh no longer for the lusts" | "You will no longer spend the rest of your earthly lives chasing after human desires" | **LOGOS** structure adopted, with EMET's "desires." PNEUMA's paraphrase ("chasing after") adds interpretive color not in the source. |

### Verse 3
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "nations" vs "Gentiles" vs "pagans" | "will of the nations" | "desire of the Gentiles" | "what the pagans love to do" | **LOGOS** — "Gentiles" is the standard formal equivalent of ta ethne. "Nations" is overly literal; "pagans" is too colloquial. |
| "desire" vs "will" | "will of the nations" | "desire of the Gentiles" | paraphrase | **LOGOS** — boulema here means "wish, desire, intention" rather than the more authoritative "will" (thelema). |
| Vice list rendering | "licentiousness, desires, drunkenness, revelries, drinking bouts, and unlawful idolatries" | "licentiousness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking bouts, and lawless idolatries" | "wild partying, unbridled lusts, drunken binges, orgies, drinking bouts, and disgusting idol worship" | **LOGOS** — maintains formal equivalence. PNEUMA's "orgies" for komois and "disgusting idol worship" for athemitais eidololatriais are over-interpretive. |

### Verse 4
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "surprised" vs "astonished" | "they are surprised" | "they are astonished" | "your former friends are surprised" | **LOGOS** — xenizontai carries the stronger sense of "astonished, taken aback." PNEUMA adds "former friends" (not in text). |
| "blaspheming" vs "revile" vs "slander" | "blaspheming" | "blaspheming" | "they slander you" | **SOPHIA mediation** — "they revile you" chosen. "Blaspheming" in modern English implies blasphemy against God; the Greek blasphemountes here means verbal abuse directed at the believers. "Slander" (PNEUMA) is close but adds "you" as object. "Revile" captures the abusive speech without the theological connotation. |

### Verse 5
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "having readiness" vs "ready" vs "stands ready" | "the [one] having readiness to judge" | "him who is ready to judge" | "the one who stands ready to judge" | **LOGOS** with PNEUMA influence — "him who stands ready to judge." EMET is too wooden; PNEUMA's "stands ready" nicely renders etoimos echonti. Adopted "stands ready." |

### Verse 6
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "good news was proclaimed" vs "gospel was preached" | "the good news was proclaimed" | "the gospel was preached" | "the Good News was preached" | **SOPHIA mediation** — "the good news was preached" chosen. "Good news" preserves the euangelion root meaning (EMET/PNEUMA); "was preached" is more natural English than "was proclaimed" for eueggelisthe. |
| "that they might be judged" construction | "that they might be judged according to men in [the] flesh, but might live according to God in [the] spirit" | "that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but might live according to God in the spirit" | "so that although they were judged in the body as all humans are, they might live in the spirit as God does" | **LOGOS** — preserves the Greek antithetical structure without PNEUMA's interpretive additions ("as all humans are," "as God does"). |

### Verse 7
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "unto prayers" vs "for prayers" | "be watchful unto prayers" | "be watchful unto prayers" | "so that you can pray effectively" | **EMET/LOGOS** — both preserve eis proseuchas. Changed "unto" to "for" for modern readability. PNEUMA's "pray effectively" adds interpretation. |
| "sober-minded" vs "clear-minded and self-controlled" | "Be sober-minded" | "Be sober-minded" | "be clear-minded and self-controlled" | **EMET/LOGOS** — sophronesate is one word; PNEUMA splits it into two concepts. "Sober-minded" is the standard rendering. |

### Verse 8
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "before all things" vs "above all" | "before all things" | "Above all things" | "Above all" | **LOGOS** — pro panton can mean "before" (temporal) or "above" (priority); "above all" captures the sense of priority Peter intends. |
| "fervent" placement | "having the love among yourselves [being] fervent" | "having fervent love among yourselves" | "love each other deeply" | **SOPHIA mediation** — "maintain fervent love among yourselves." LOGOS order is best; added "maintain" to render the participial echontes more actively. PNEUMA's "deeply" loses the specific meaning of ektene ("stretched out, fervent"). |

### Verse 10
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "among yourselves" vs "to one another" | "ministering it among yourselves" | "minister it to one another" | "Use it to serve one another" | **LOGOS** — eis heautous means "to/for one another." PNEUMA's paraphrase is too free. |
| "just as he received" vs "as each one has received" | "each [one] just as he received a gift" | "As each one has received a gift" | "Each of you has received a gift from God" | **LOGOS** — PNEUMA adds "from God" (not explicit in text at this point). |

### Verse 11
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "the might" vs "the dominion" vs "the power" | "the might" (to kratos) | "the dominion" | "the power" | **LOGOS** — kratos specifically denotes sovereign strength or dominion, distinct from dunamis ("power"). "Dominion" is the most precise English rendering. |
| "Yeshua Christos" vs "Jesus Christ" | "Yeshua Christos" | "Jesus Christ" | "Jesus Christ" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "Jesus Christ." While EMET's transliteration is defensible, the consensus uses standard English forms for proper names throughout. |

### Verse 12
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "testing" vs "trial" vs "to test you" | "for [a] testing" | "for a trial" | "to test you" | **SOPHIA mediation** — "for a testing." The Greek pros peirasmon is a noun ("testing/trial"), not a verb. "Testing" preserves the noun form and echoes 1 Peter 1:7 (refining fire imagery). |
| "burning" vs "fiery ordeal" | "the burning among you" | "the burning among you" | "the fiery ordeal" | **EMET/LOGOS** — purosei literally means "burning." PNEUMA's "fiery ordeal" is interpretive. |

### Verse 13
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "exulting" vs "with exultation" vs "overjoyed" | "rejoice, exulting" | "rejoice with exultation" | "may be overjoyed" | **LOGOS** — agalliomenoi is a participle conveying manner; "with exultation" is formally precise. PNEUMA loses the dual-joy structure. |

### Verse 14
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "reproached" vs "insulted" | "If you are reproached" | "If you are reproached" | "If you are insulted" | **EMET/LOGOS** — oneidizesthe means "reproached, reviled." "Reproached" is more formally accurate than "insulted." |
| Spirit clause | "the [Spirit] of the glory and the [Spirit] of the God upon you rests" | "the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you" | "the Spirit of glory — the Spirit of God — rests upon you" | **LOGOS** — clean, accurate. EMET's double bracketed [Spirit] is too mechanical; PNEUMA's dash construction is interpretive. |

### Verse 16
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "the God" vs "God" | "let him glorify the God" | "let him glorify God" | "praise God" | **LOGOS** — English does not use the article with "God" as a proper noun. EMET's "the God" is overliteral. |
| "in this name" | "in this name" | "in this name" | "that you bear that name" | **EMET/LOGOS** — both preserve the Sinaiticus variant en to onomati touto. PNEUMA paraphrases away the distinctive reading. |

### Verse 17
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "good news" vs "gospel" | "the good news of the God" | "the gospel of God" | "God's Good News" | **SOPHIA mediation** — "the good news of God." Consistent with v.6 ruling to use "good news" for euangelion throughout. |

### Verse 19
| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| "So then, also let" vs "Therefore let" | "So then, also let the [ones] suffering" | "Therefore let those who suffer" | "So then, those who suffer...should entrust" | **LOGOS** — "Therefore let" is crisp and carries the inferential force of hoste kai. |
| "the souls of them" vs "their souls" vs "their lives" | "the souls of them" | "their souls" | "their lives" | **LOGOS** — psuchas means "souls." PNEUMA's "lives" loses the theological weight. EMET's word order is too literal. |

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Total disagreements logged | 32 |
| EMET (literal) rulings adopted | 3 |
| LOGOS (formal) rulings adopted | 18 |
| PNEUMA (dynamic) rulings adopted | 0 |
| EMET/LOGOS agreement adopted | 5 |
| SOPHIA mediation (blended) | 6 |

## General Observations

1. **LOGOS dominated this chapter** — 1 Peter 4 is theologically dense, with precise doctrinal formulations (vv.1, 6, 14, 17) where formal equivalence best preserves the author's intent. LOGOS consistently struck the right balance between accuracy and readability.

2. **PNEUMA's expansions were frequently unsupported** — additions like "he had" (v.1), "former friends" (v.4), "as all humans are" (v.6), and "from God" (v.10) insert interpretive material not present in the Sinaiticus text. While clarifying, they cross the line from translation to commentary.

3. **EMET's mechanical literalism** — constructions like "the [ones] suffering," "the souls of them," and "the God" accurately mirror the Greek but produce English that obscures rather than communicates. EMET's textual notes, however, were invaluable for variant tracking.

4. **Key Sinaiticus variants preserved** — the omission of "for us/you" in v.1, "in this name" (not "in this matter") in v.16, and the shorter reading without the added clause in v.14 all follow the Sinaiticus text faithfully.

5. **"Good news" over "gospel"** — SOPHIA adopted "good news" consistently (vv.6, 17) to preserve the euangelion root meaning, following EMET/PNEUMA against LOGOS's "gospel." While "gospel" is a venerable English word, "good news" makes the Greek meaning transparent to modern readers.
